title: Experimental Demonstration of Free-Space Unidimensional Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution Under High Detector Noise
authors: Rachita Nandan, Jayanth Ramakrishnan, Shashi Prabhakar, R. P. Singh
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD), which uses quadratures of the electromagnetic field, enables practical quantum communication using standard telecommunication technologies. Unidimensional CV-QKD (UD-CVQKD) simplifies the implementation by restricting modulation to a single quadrature. In this work, we experimentally demonstrate a free-space Gaussian-modulated UD-CVQKD system operating under a high detector electronic-noise regime (1.4 shot-noise units). The system employs polarized coherent states with signal and local oscillator co-propagating in the same spatial mode in orthogonal polarizations, ensuring stable interference. System security is analyzed under both untrusted (UTD) and trusted (TD) detector noise models. While no positive secret key rate is obtained under the UTD model, the TD model enables secure key generation over a finite range of modulation variances, highlighting the critical role of detector trust in high-noise conditions. A maximum secret key rate of 270 kbps is achieved at an optimal modulation variance of 11.57. Furthermore, secure operation requires high-transmittance (low-loss) channels under such noise conditions. This study demonstrates the practical feasibility of free-space UD-CVQKD in realistic high electronic-noise detection constraints and highlights detector electronic noise as a key limiting factor in practical systems.
summary: The authors experimentally realized Unidimensional CV-QKD over free space, proving its feasibility despite significant detector electronic noise. They found that secure key generation is only possible when assuming the detector is trusted, achieving a rate of 270 kbps. This work underscores that practical quantum communication systems must account for and mitigate realistic noise sources like detector electronics.
main_problem: The paper experimentally demonstrates Unidimensional Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution (UD-CVQKD) in a free-space setting while operating under challenging conditions of high detector electronic noise.
main_result: Secure key generation was achieved using the Trusted Detector model, reaching a maximum secret key rate of 270 kbps at an optimal modulation variance. The study highlights that detector trust is critical, as the Untrusted Detector model failed to yield positive rates under these noisy conditions.
method: The security analysis employed worst-case assumptions over unknown correlation parameters ($C_p$) using established key rate formulas derived from mutual information and Eve's accessible information.
model_or_system: The system uses polarized coherent states transmitted through a free-space channel. The protocol is simplified to modulate only one quadrature (UD-CVQKD) for implementation simplicity.
key_observables: Secret key rate ($K_{TD}$), measured quadratures ($\hat{X}, \hat{P}$), and the secure region in parameter space $(V_{B_p}, C_p)$.
important_parameters: High detector electronic noise (1.4 shot-noise units), modulation variance ($V_A$), and channel transmittance ($T_x$).
main_assumptions: The analysis critically depends on the assumption of a Trusted Detector model to ensure positive key rates; performance is limited by low-loss channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the physical and secure operational regions in parameter space for both trusted (TD) and untrusted (UTD) detector models, showing how high noise shrinks the secure region significantly.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from describing the physical setup and measurement techniques to deriving security bounds under different noise assumptions. It then numerically analyzes the key rate dependence on modulation variance and channel loss, culminating in experimental validation of feasibility.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, high-noise experimental benchmark for quantum communication protocols, directly addressing practical limitations (detector noise) that often plague theoretical models in quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Physical and secure regions in the (V B p , Cp) parameter space for both TD and UTD models. The solid black curve denotes the physicality boundary. The red and blue dashed curves represent the zero key-rate (K = 0) boundaries for the UTD and TD cases, respectively, separating secure (K > 0) and unsecure (K < 0) regions, shown in green and red shades. The region between the vertical dotted lines indicates the Cp-independent security threshold for both models. Results are shown for a low electronic-noise detector with Vel = 0.1 SNU. (b) Same as in (a), but for a high electronic-noise detector with Vel = 1.4 SNU. For higher Vel, this Cp-independent secure region shrinks...

FIG. 2. Behavior of the mutual information IAB, Holevo bound χBE, and TD secret key rate KTD as functions of the modulation variance VA. The parameters are V B p = 1.003, Tx = 0.91, ξx = 0.01 SNU, η = 0.81, and Vel = 1.4 SNU, assuming worst-case Cp.

FIG. 3. Schematic of the experimental setup for Gaussian-modulated UD-CVQKD system over a free-space channel. HWP: half-wave plate; PBS: polarizing beam-splitter; V-NDF: variable neutral density filter; M: mirror; AM: amplitude modulator; QWP: quarter-wave plate; AWG: arbitrary waveform generator; Q-H-Q: QWP-HWP-QWP configuration; PrM: prism mirror; L: lens; BHD: balanced homodyne detector; MSO: mixed signal oscilloscope. Inset: phase-space representation of unidimensional Gaussian modulation in the X-quadrature.

FIG. 4. Calibration of the amplitude modulation and de- tection system. (a) Mean BHD output as a function of the applied AWG voltage (-2.25 V to +2.25 V), exhibiting a lin- ear response for the modulated X-quadrature, while the P- quadrature remains approximately constant. (b) Correspond- ing variance with AWG voltage, showing a quadratic depen- dence for X and nearly constant response for P.

FIG. 5. Quadrature probability distributions for Alice and Bob at VA = 17.54 SNU. Alice’s distribution is Gaussian with variance set by the applied modulation, while Bob’s measured distribution shows reduced variance (V B x = 16.73 SNU) due to channel loss.
